title: File I/O
summary: Read and write files using Java's file I/O classes, handle encoding and exceptions

sections.title: File I/O as Reading a Recipe Book
sections.content: Reading a file is like opening a recipe book, copying a recipe onto a notepad, and closing the book. First you open the file (create a connection), then you read the data line by line (like copying text), and finally you close the file to tell the system you're done. Writing a file is the reverse: you open a blank notebook, write your data, and close it. The important rule is always close your files when finished, like turning off the stove after cooking. Java's try-with-resources handles this automatically, closing the file even if an exception occurs.

sections.title: Reading a File with try-with-resources
sections.content: This code reads a file named 'scores.csv' line by line and prints each line

sections.title: BufferedReader vs Files.readAllLines
sections.content: Files.readAllLines is convenient but loads the entire file into memory at once. For a small config file, this is fine. For a 2 GB log file, this will crash your program with an OutOfMemoryError. That's where BufferedReader comes in — it reads one line at a time, keeping only the current line in memory. Think of it as drinking from a water fountain (one sip at a time) instead of trying to swallow the entire reservoir. For large files, always use BufferedReader with a while loop reading line by line.

sections.title: Not closing file resources, causing resource leaks
sections.content: Most common mistake: opening a FileReader or Scanner on a file but not calling close() when done. Open files consume operating system resources, and your program can run out of file handles. Fix: always use try-with-resources (try (Scanner sc = new Scanner(new File("data.txt"))) { ... }) which auto-closes. Wrong: Scanner sc = new Scanner(new File("data.txt")); /* no close */ Correct: try (Scanner sc = new Scanner(new File("data.txt"))) { ... }

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Write a program that reads a text file called 'temperatures.txt' where each line contains a city name and temperature separated by a comma (e.g., 'Madrid,28.5'). Calculate the average temperature across all cities and print it. Handle the case where the file doesn't exist by printing a friendly error message.
hints: What method splits a String into parts using a delimiter?
How do you parse a temperature like '28.5' into a double?
What exception does Files.readAllLines throw if the file is missing?
